name: Distillery District
details: The brick-lined Victorian streets of the Gooderham & Worts Distillery are now a popular car-free district of art galleries, restaurants, brewpubs, coffee shops, and other independent businesses. On summer weekends, the district hosts themed festivals and live music. The Distillery Winter Village holiday market attracts over a million visitors annually.

name: First Parliament Site
details: Nothing remains here but a plaque honouring the first parliament buildings for Upper Canada (now Ontario) that stood on this site from 1798 to 1832. During the War of 1812, occupying American troops set fire to the buildings. The British responded a year later by torching what is now the White House in Washington, DC.

name: The Esplanade
details: In the early 1900s, after infill pushed the shoreline of Lake Ontario south from Front Street, the Esplanade was an industrial area. David Crombie Park, which runs for six blocks along the Esplanade, now sits atop former railway sidings. Crombie, mayor of Toronto in the 1970s, oversaw a major redevelopment of the St. Lawrence Market area. Vacant warehouses have given way to a vibrant walkable community of co-ops, condos, schools, and businesses.

name: St. Lawrence Market South
details: The St. Lawrence Market south building has hogged this corner since 1842, when the central section served as City Hall. The 120 vendors across two floors include butchers, fishmongers, organic vegetable stalls, take-out restaurants, and speciality shops. The South Market is open every day of the week except Mondays.

name: Gooderham Flatiron Building
details: Often described as the most-photographed building in Toronto, the Flatiron has stood in the heart of Old Toronto since 1892. George Gooderham of the Gooderham & Worts Distillery once kept a watchful eye on his sprawling business empire from an  office in the top floor cupola. Today there's a pub in the basement, with a sunny sidewalk patio in the summers.
note: Note: As of spring 2026, this building remains under scaffolding for extensive restoration. The pub is still operating. Stay tuned!
photoLicense: licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution-Share Alike 4.0 International license

name: Berczy Park
details: Berczy Park became a social media favourite in 2017 when it was refurbished with a new Claude Cormier-designed fountain featuring 27 cast iron dogs, and one cat. Look very closely and you'll realize what the cat is staring at. Also, don't miss the distinctive Derek Michael Besant mural which has decorated the rear wall of the neighbouring Flatiron Building since 1980.

name: Hockey Hall of Fame
details: This national hockey museum curates exhibits and memorabilia related to Canada's game. Olympians, NHLers, PWHLers and junior players are all honoured. The Stanley Cup lives here, too.
photoLicense: licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution-Share Alike 4.0 International license

name: Union Station
details: Union Station has been Toronto's transit hub since 1927. National and regional trains, buses, and subways all converge here. The Great Hall on the main level near Front Street has been freshly renovated to show off its soaring ceiling and Beaux-Arts finishes. The lower levels are a warren of speciality shops, international food courts, and restaurants.
photoLicense: licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution 2.0 Generic license

name: Maple Leaf Square
details: This normally bland area where Union Station meets Scotiabank Arena becomes the official outdoor gathering spot for hardy fans of the Toronto Maple Leafs and Toronto Raptors during playoff runs. When the Raptors are playing, the square is known as Jurrasic Park.
photoLicense: licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution 2.0 Generic license

name: Scotiabank Arena
details: Scotiabank Arena is the shared home of the Toronto Maple Leafs hockey team and the Toronto Raptors basketball team, as well as a popular venue for major concerts.
photoLicense: licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution 2.0 Generic license

name: Roundhouse Park
details: The John Street Roundhouse was built in 1929 by the Canadian Pacific Railway Company. Closed in the 1980s and converted into a park in 1997, its massive locomotive turntable still functions. The adjoining train repair sheds now house the Toronto Railway Museum, a brewpub, and a restaurant arcade.
photoLicense: licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution-Share Alike 3.0 Unported license

name: Ripley's Aquarium
details: Canada's largest indoor aquarium opened in 2013 and quickly became a central attraction for tourists and locals alike. Highlights include photogenic neon jellyfish and a 96-metre-long underwater tunnel.
photoLicense: published under the Creative Commons CC0 1.0 Universal Public Domain Dedication

name: Rogers Centre
details: Home of Canada's only Major League Baseball team, the Toronto Blue Jays. When the building opened in 1989 as SkyDome, its retractable roof was revolutionary. It still keeps fans and players comfortable from April to October.

name: CN Tower
details: One of Toronto's most recognizable symbols, the CN Tower was the world's tallest free-standing structure from 1975 to 1997. On a clear day, it's possible to glimpse Niagara Falls and New York State from the observation deck.

name: King Edward Hotel
details: The King Edward Hotel has long been one of the more prestigious hotels in Toronto, with a list of famous past guests that includes Queen Elizabeth II, Mark Twain, Elvis Presley, the Beatles, Teddy Roosevelt, and the Dalai Lama. The King Eddy's weekend Afternoon Tea is a tradition that dates back to 1903.

name: Toronto Street
details: A bank stands on the northeast corner of King Street East and Toronto Street today, but in the 1830s it was dominated by the King Street Gaol. Just outside the two-story brick building stood the gallows where Samuel Lount and Peter Matthews were hanged for their roles in the Upper Canada Rebellion, a failed uprising against the colonial British government in 1837.

name: Adelaide Street Court House
details: The stern columns of this court house have loomed over Adelaide Street since 1852. After the courts moved to City Hall in 1900, the building was home to the Arts and Letters Club. This men-only organization hosted painters, writers, and musicians until 1913, including the landscape artists who would later form the Group of Seven. To see the interior, you can eat at Italian restaurant now using the space.

name: St. James Cathedral
details: The Cathedral of St. James is the fourth Anglican church to stand at this location. It opened in 1853 after the previous structure burned in the Great Fire of 1849. When the elaborate tower and spires were added in the 1870s it became the tallest structure in Canada, and kept that status for 25 years. On Sunday mornings, the tower's full set of 12 bells are rung in mesmerizing sequence by the Guild of Change Ringers.

name: St. James Park
details: At the south end of St. James Park there is a formal garden with a fountain ringed by carefully-tended flowers and plantings. In the center of the park is a stylish new timber pavilion for concerts and festivals. Meanwhile, few parkgoers realize that the grassy field next to the church conceals the bones of thousands of Torontonians frantically buried in mass graves during two cholera epidemics in the 1830s.

name: Christie, Brown & Co. Factory
details: Built in 1868, the Christie, Brown & Co. Biscuit Factory eventually became the largest biscuit manufacturer in Canada, using newfangled steam-powered machines to churn out the biscuits they shipped around the British Empire. Today, the building is part of the George Brown College campus.

name: Toronto's First Post Office
details: When it opened in 1833, Toronto's first post office was run by British Royal Mail. Today, it is a National Historic Site operated by the York Historical Society as both a museum and a modern Canada Post outlet. Passing through the tiny door onto the creaky wooden floors is like stepping back in time. The museum exhibits are free, but donations are appreciated.

name: Toronto Railway Company Stables
details: The building was originally constructed in 1888 as stables for the Toronto Railway Company, an operator of horse-drawn streetcars in the pre-electrification era. This structure used to be much larger: records from 1891 show it housed 495 horses. In 1977, the Young People's Theatre purchased and restored the property into a venue with two stages.

name: St. Lawrence Market North
details: Years late and millions over budget, the eye-catching new St. Lawrence Market North building finally opened to the public in 2025. It currently sits empty most of the week, but on Saturday mornings it comes alive as farmers and specialty food producers arrive from the surrounding towns and rural areas to sell their high-quality products to crowds of hungry Torontonians.

name: St. Lawrence Hall
details: From 1850, St. Lawrence Hall was the centre of Toronto social life, serving as a venue for meetings, speeches, concerts and elegant balls. The hall also hosted numerous anti-slavery conferences promoting abolition in the United States, including an 1854 speech by social reformer Frederick Douglass. In 1867, the Fathers of Confederation, including our first Prime Minister John A. Macdonald, spoke here as Canada was born.
photoLicense: licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution-Share Alike 4.0 International license

name: Toronto Sculpture Garden
details: Since 1981, the Toronto Sculpture Garden has exhibited a wide range of creative artworks in a small park tucked between two circa-1840s heritage buildings. In the summer, there's a waterfall fountain. The artwork typically changes once or twice per year, with over 80 artists featured to date.

name: Indigenous Hub
details: This mixed-use centre offers educational services and employment training alongside retail space and rental housing. At its heart is the Anishnawbe Health Centre, which provides both conventional care and traditional land-based Indigenous healing. Outside stands In Equilibrium, a fringed sculpture by Ludovic Boney, a Quebec-based artist of Wendat descent.

name: Former Canary Restaurant
details: When this ornate brick building opened in 1859 as the Palace Street School, it had two classrooms, one for girls and one for boys. After 1900, the building operated as a hotel. But many current Torontonians of a certain age remember it as the Canary Restaurant, a greasy-spoon diner that was so well-loved it gave the Canary District its name. Now freshly restored and painted a vibrant red, the building is awaiting its next identity.

name: Front Street Promenade
details: The Front Street Promenade is the pedestrian-friendly main passage through the Canary District. It features plenty of trees, benches, and artwork, along with a splash pad for kids and dogs. Everything here is barely a decade old, built on remediated former industrial lands, in part with funding obtained for the 2015 Pan Am Games. Once a wasteland, the Canary District is now home to thousands of residents.

name: Garden of Future Follies
details: The Garden of Future Follies is a sculptural artwork by artist Hadley Howes. The artist created impressions from over 80 other sculptures and architectural features located around Toronto, cast them in bronze, and recombined them in ways their original creators could never have imagined.

name: Corktown Common
details: The trails of Corktown Common meander through thick forests, marshes, ponds, open lawns, and a children's playground. It's not hard to lose sight of the city skyline here, surrounded only by croaking bullfrogs and swooping red-winged blackbirds. The park also serves an important hidden purpose: it was carefully engineered as a flood berm to protect the surrounding neighbourhoods from a once-in-500-years flood of the Don River.

name: Don River
details: You may catch glimpses of the Don River from Corktown Common. Archaeology suggests Indigenous peoples settled in the Don Valley more than 6,000 years ago. Europeans later used the river as the eastern boundary of the original city of Toronto. Like many urban rivers, the Don was abused by industry in the 1900s, polluted and boxed in with concrete. Today, it's being remediated, with a newly naturalized mouth opening into Lake Ontario just south of here.

name: Lawren Harris Square
details: This gravelly Parisian-style square is named after Canadian painter Lawren Harris (1885-1970). Harris was a member of the famous Group of Seven, a collective known for their bold and modern Canadian wilderness landscapes. Though the artists often painted in remote areas, they met in Toronto, and Harris painted many Toronto streetscapes early in his career.

name: Underpass Park
details: This previously unused space was repurposed in 2015. There's a skate park, basketball hoops, colourful public art, and a children's playground. On Thursdays in the summer, a farmer's market fills the open space on the west side of St. Lawrence Street. Many were skeptical, but Underpass Park has proven to be well used. The space has also had numerous cameo appearances in TV commercials.
note: Note: As of spring 2026, these areas are closed while the expressway ramps above undergo maintenance. Stay tuned!

name: Riverside Church
details: Lorem

name: Bright Street
details: Lorem

name: Sackville Playground
details: Lorem

name: Inglenook School
details: Lorem

name: Blackburn House Site
details: Lorem

name: Enoch Turner Schoolhouse
details: The construction of this schoolhouse in 1848 was funded by local brewer Enoch Turner, who saw that many of his Irish immigrant employees were unable to afford the fees needed to send their children to other schools. The building is now run by a charitable foundation, and is often used for weddings. The public can vist for free during the city's annual Doors Open event held every May.

name: Little Trinity Church
details: Opened in 1844, Little Trinity Anglican Church is the oldest church building still used for worship in Toronto. Most of the original congregation were local immigrant workers from the Corktown area who were unable to afford the high 'pew fees' at the larger St. James Cathedral near St. Lawrence Market.

name: Dominion Foundries
details: Dominion Wheel & Foundries built this industrial complex in the early 1900s to supply railway companies with wheels and other parts for their rolling stock. Various plans to repurpose the large, light-filled spaces have been proposed. But in 2021, without consultation, the Ontario government deployed crews to demolish the site. Angry protestors soon arrived, and courts paused the demolition. The land is now in limbo, with bulldozed areas used as a parking lot.

name: Cube Houses
details: These three unusual houses in the form of rotated cubes were built in 1996, based on similar homes designed by architect Piet Blom in the city of Rotterdam. For decades, they've been a curiosities, visible from nearby expressway ramps. Artists, musicians, and other creatives have made them home. The future of the structures is in doubt, however, with the land slated for major redevelopment.

name: Cooper Koo YMCA
details: This family-oriented YMCA, with facilities for both kids and adults, was the first building to open in the new Canary District. During the 2015 Pan Am Games, when it was part of the Athlete's Village, the gym facilities were used by competitors training for their events. Now it serves the community, with two swimming pools, an indoor running track, a gymnasium, and a green roof.

name: St. Paul's Basilica
details: The oldest Catholic church in Toronto, the congregation has been meeting at this location since 1824. The current building was constructed in 1889, with the Italian-style campanile added in 1905, containing the bell from the original church.  Pope John Paul II designated this church a Minor Basilica in 1999.

name: Dominion Hotel
details: Lorem

name: Dominion Brewery
details: Brewer Robert T. Davies founded the Dominion Brewery in 1877 after leaving his previous job at his brother's Don Brewery a few blocks east. The brewery's name refers to the Dominion of Canada: not yet an independent country but merely a part of the British Empire. The brewery complex, with its large entry arches, was incorporated into a modern retail and office development in the 1980s.

name: Corktown Overpasses
details: Like the east sides of many major North American cities, Corktown was riven in two by new highway overpasses constructed in the 1960s, to provide drivers with acccess to the Don Valley Parkway. Home at the time to poorer and working class people, the area was not able to successfully mobilize against the intrusion, as residents of the Annex did in the western part of the city.

name: Heritage Commercial Rows
details: In 2020, the Toronto Preservation Board recommended many properies in the Corktown area be added to the City of Toronto's Heritage Register. Among them were these 1800s commercial row blocks, featuring retail business on the ground floor and living accommodations above, often with steep gabled rooflines.

name: 51 Division
details: The Toronto Police Service 51 Division Headquarters operate out of this repurposed 1898 building, originally constructed by the Consumers Gas Company. The building was vacant for nearly 50 years before being completely restored and modernized in 2004.

name: Data Centre
details: The Parliament Street Data Centre, completed in 2015, is a sharp contrast to the many historic buildings in this area. The textured facade was designed to mimic an old-school computer punchcard. This secure facility houses the digital infrastructure used by many Toronto institutions, with dedicated connections to Toronto's City Hall and financial core to ensure uptime even during a major disaster.

name: Gilead Place
details: This narrow laneway is a microcosm of the changes that have transformed Corktown. In the 1800s Gilead Place was lined with ramshackle rowhouses, home to labourers at the nearby factories and breweries. By the 1930s these homes were being demolished in favour of brick industrial buildings. Today, it's come a full circle, with expensive new townhomes and condos replacing the industry.
photoLicense: in the public domain. Made available
